person.name: Joan Skelton
person.alternateName: Joan Skelton Lady Middleton of East Swinburne
person.userCertifiedLifeSummary: Joan (d. 17 Mar. 1450), d. & coh. of Sir Clement Skelton of Stainton and Orton & Joan, d/o Giles Orton; married twice. First to Sir John Blennerhasset (fl. 1394), then to Sir John Middleton of Belsay, co. Northumb (b. 1373 - d. 1441).

sourceList.title: SKELTON, Sir Clement (b. bef. 1345), of Stainton and Orton, Cumb. HOP. Web.
MIDDLETON, Sir John (c.1373-1441), of Belsay, Northumb. HOP

nameMeanings.name: Skelton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Yorkshire): habitational name mainly from Skelton in Cumberland, or any of various places called Skelton in Yorkshire, North Yorkshire, and East Yorkshire. The placenames probably all derive from Old English scelf ‘rock, ledge, shelf’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’, with initial Sk-, instead of Sh-, due to Scandinavian influence. However, in some cases, the first element may be a lost river name, from Old Norse skjallr ‘resounding’; compare Skelding . This English surname has been established in Queen's County [Laois], Ireland, since the 16th century.

variants.name: Skelding
meanings: English: habitational name from Skelding (Yorkshire). The place takes its name from the river Skell, itself from Old Norse skjallr ‘resounding’, + Old English denu ‘valley’. In addition, the name may come from Skeldon (Ayrshire), although there is a lack of evidence for a historic presence in this part of Scotland.

variants.name: Skilton
meanings: English (Surrey): probably a variant of Skelton , though in some cases the name may be a variant of Shilton, a habitational name from Shilton (Warwickshire, Oxfordshire) or Earl Shilton (Leicestershire), from Old English scelf, scylf ‘rock, ledge, shelf’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’.

nameMeanings.name: Joan
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Contracted form of Old French Jo(h)anne, from Latin Io(h)anna ( see Joanna ). In England this was the usual feminine form of John from the Middle English period onwards and was extremely popular, but in the 16th and 17th centuries it steadily lost ground to Jane . It was strongly revived in the first part of the 20th century, partly under the influence of George Bernard Shaw's play St Joan ( 1923 ), based on the life of Joan of Arc ( 1412–31 ). Claiming to be guided by the voices of the saints, she persuaded the French dauphin to defy the occupying English forces and have himself crowned, and she led the French army that raised the siege of Orleans in 1429 . The following year she was captured by the Burgundians and sold to the English, and a year later she was burned at the stake for witchcraft at the age of 18 or 19. Her story has captured the imagination of many writers, and she is variously portrayed as a national and political hero, a model of apolitical straightforwardness and honesty, and a religious heroine. She was canonized in 1920 . More recent influences have included the American film actress Joan Crawford ( 1908–77 , born Lucille le Sueur ), the British actress Joan Collins ( b. 1933 ), the American comedienne Joan Rivers ( b. 1933 ), and the West Indian pop singer Joan Armatrading ( b. 1950 ).

variants.name: John
meanings: Greek: the Lord gave graciously; whom Jehovah gave; whom Jehovah bestowed.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: Joan Skelton was born as the daughter of Sir Clement Skelton MP and Joan Orton. She married Sir John Middleton MP before October 1409. They were the parents of at least 4 sons. She died on 17 March 1450, in Cumberland, England, United Kingdom.
